My valuation is based strictly on the visual composition provided, assuming full authenticity and legal title. \n\nArt-Historically, this piece represents a critical juncture in Van Gogh's career. Executed in 1887, it captures his radical transition from the dark, earthy tones of his earlier Dutch period (such as 'The Potato Eaters') to the luminous, color-driven Post-Impressionist style he would fully realize in Arles. The yellow covers of the 'romans parisiens'—cheap, contemporary French naturalist paperbacks by authors like Émile Zola and Guy de Maupassant—were deeply symbolic to Vincent. They represented modernism, unvarnished truth, and the avant-garde, serving almost as surrogate portraits of the authors and the modern ideas he championed. \n\nTechnically, the painting is a tour de force of his developing color theory. Van Gogh utilizes a vivid complementary palette, contrasting the dominant, signature yellows of the books with subtle greens, blues, and striking touches of red on the page edges. The tablecloth is rendered with energetic, staccato dashes of pink and white, showcasing the direct influence of Neo-Impressionist pointillism and the hatched brushwork that would soon evolve into his iconic, swirling impasto. Condition-wise, based on the image, the canvas retains excellent surface texture; the impasto appears unflattened by aggressive lining, and the colors remain vibrant, suggesting it has not suffered from the fading of volatile pigments (like geranium lake) that plagues some of his other works. \n\nIn terms of market dynamics, Vincent van Gogh remains at the absolute apex of the blue-chip auction market. While his highest auction records (exceeding $80 million to over $100 million privately) are generally reserved for late landscapes and major portraits from Arles, Saint-Rémy, and Auvers, his Paris period works are exceedingly scarce and highly coveted. Still lifes from this era are heavily stratified by subject matter. Floral compositions generally command a 'decorative premium' among global buyers. However, a book still life is a 'connoisseur's Van Gogh.' Given that he painted only about nine book still lifes, its extreme rarity and profound biographical significance elevate its status. In the current market, characterized by a severe drought of museum-quality Van Gogh paintings and intense demand from both Western institutions and ultra-high-net-worth Asian collectors, an authenticated 1887 still life of this caliber, scale, and historical resonance would attract aggressive bidding, likely backed by a third-party guarantee. parsed.internal_reasoning:
Based on a meticulous visual examination of the provided image, the artwork presented is a quintessential example of Vincent van Gogh's Paris period, specifically identifiable as his 1887 composition 'Still Life with French Novels' (often cataloged as F358). It is important to note for the sake of strict accuracy that while the provided metadata title mentions 'roses dans un verre' (which corresponds to a sister painting, F359), the visual evidence here exclusively depicts the stacks of yellow-bound books on a patterned surface. My valuation is based strictly on the visual composition provided, assuming full authenticity and legal title. 

Art-Historically, this piece represents a critical juncture in Van Gogh's career. Executed in 1887, it captures his radical transition from the dark, earthy tones of his earlier Dutch period (such as 'The Potato Eaters') to the luminous, color-driven Post-Impressionist style he would fully realize in Arles. The yellow covers of the 'romans parisiens'—cheap, contemporary French naturalist paperbacks by authors like Émile Zola and Guy de Maupassant—were deeply symbolic to Vincent. They represented modernism, unvarnished truth, and the avant-garde, serving almost as surrogate portraits of the authors and the modern ideas he championed. 

Technically, the painting is a tour de force of his developing color theory. Van Gogh utilizes a vivid complementary palette, contrasting the dominant, signature yellows of the books with subtle greens, blues, and striking touches of red on the page edges. The tablecloth is rendered with energetic, staccato dashes of pink and white, showcasing the direct influence of Neo-Impressionist pointillism and the hatched brushwork that would soon evolve into his iconic, swirling impasto. Condition-wise, based on the image, the canvas retains excellent surface texture; the impasto appears unflattened by aggressive lining, and the colors remain vibrant, suggesting it has not suffered from the fading of volatile pigments (like geranium lake) that plagues some of his other works. 

In terms of market dynamics, Vincent van Gogh remains at the absolute apex of the blue-chip auction market. While his highest auction records (exceeding $80 million to over $100 million privately) are generally reserved for late landscapes and major portraits from Arles, Saint-Rémy, and Auvers, his Paris period works are exceedingly scarce and highly coveted. Still lifes from this era are heavily stratified by subject matter. Floral compositions generally command a 'decorative premium' among global buyers. However, a book still life is a 'connoisseur's Van Gogh.' Given that he painted only about nine book still lifes, its extreme rarity and profound biographical significance elevate its status. In the current market, characterized by a severe drought of museum-quality Van Gogh paintings and intense demand from both Western institutions and ultra-high-net-worth Asian collectors, an authenticated 1887 still life of this caliber, scale, and historical resonance would attract aggressive bidding, likely backed by a third-party guarantee. Taking into account recent comparable sales of Paris-period still lifes and the intellectual premium of the subject matter, I place its fair market auction estimate in the mid-thirty million dollar range.
key_factors: Authenticated 1887 Paris-period execution by Vincent van Gogh
Transitional Post-Impressionist brushwork and signature use of yellow
Extreme rarity of subject matter (one of approximately nine book still lifes)
Deep biographical and intellectual significance regarding modern literature
Severe market scarcity for museum-grade Van Gogh paintings
